title: Recognizing the historic abolitionist events surrounding the Pearl, and honoring its legacy in American history.
plain_summary: This bill honors 77 enslaved people who tried to escape slavery in 1848 by sailing on a ship called the Pearl. The resolution recognizes how important this escape attempt was in American history. It also honors the people who helped with the escape plan. The bill wants to make sure people remember this brave attempt to gain freedom from slavery.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

sections.title: Resolution Text
sections.text: On April sixteenth, eighteen forty-eight, seventy-seven enslaved people tried to escape slavery in Washington D.C. and Alexandria, Virginia. They boarded a sailing ship called the Pearl that was waiting for them in the Potomac River. Three white men had brought the ship to Washington D.C. on April fourteenth. Daniel Drayton, who was the captain and owner of the Pearl, rented the ship for one hundred dollars and planned the trip for the enslaved people. The escape was started by two free Black men named Paul Jennings, who had been enslaved by President James Madison, and Paul Edmonson. A white person who opposed slavery named William Chaplin helped connect the enslaved people with Drayton. Another person who opposed slavery, Gerrit Smith from New York, paid for the escape. The seventy-seven enslaved people, including men, women, and children, sailed on the Pearl down the Potomac River and into the Chesapeake Bay looking for freedom. On April seventeenth, eighteen forty-eight, many slave owners in Washington D.C. realized their enslaved people had run away. They sent a group of thirty-five men to find the Pearl. This group caught up with the Pearl near Point Lookout, Maryland, and brought the enslaved people and the ship back to Washington D.C. People who supported slavery formed an angry crowd and attacked both the white people who opposed slavery and free Black people in Washington D.C. This became known as the Washington Riot of eighteen forty-eight. The slave owners sold the escaped enslaved people to traders who took them to New Orleans, Louisiana. Two of the escaped enslaved people were bought and freed through efforts led by Henry Ward Beecher's Plymouth Congregational Church in Brooklyn, New York. Drayton and two other ship workers were arrested for helping with the escape. Congressman Horace Mann represented them in court. Two of the men, including Drayton, spent four years in prison before President Millard Fillmore pardoned them based on a recommendation from Senator Charles Sumner. This escape is said to be the largest known escape by enslaved people in American history. It is also said to have inspired Harriet Beecher Stowe when she wrote the novel Uncle Tom's Cabin. Therefore, the House of Representatives honors the memory of all those who worked for freedom from slavery and all those involved in the historic events surrounding the Pearl. The House recognizes how important these events are to the history of Washington D.C. and to American history.
